meta.title: What's happening
meta.note: Curated cross-domain event log — the developments moving Mexico, each rewritten in plain English, dated, and linked to a first-party or record source. Auto-generated from the news wire on every refresh; raw or non-English items are quarantined, never published.

events.title: Former Baja California governor Ernesto Ruffo arrested on organized crime charges
events.why: Prosecutors said the arrest, tied to a fuel-theft investigation into his company, followed a high-complexity probe; a business partner was also detained.
events.background: Ruffo governed Baja California from 1989 to 1995 and later founded the customs company Ingemar, which he has said only handled customs paperwork.
events.drivers: The FGR moved after what it called a high-complexity investigation into Ingemar tied to fuel theft, detaining Ruffo and his business partner Ricardo Thompson Navarro in Ensenada.
events.implications: A former governor now faces organized crime and fuel-theft charges, tying a past state leader directly to the huachicol fiscal network federal prosecutors are targeting.
events.next: Ruffo has been transferred, handcuffed, to FGR facilities in Tijuana.

events.title: Honda halts sales of its Mexico-made electric SUV in the US
events.why: Honda will pull the Prologue EV from the US market and shift toward hybrids, citing weak electric vehicle demand, a setback for Mexico's EV manufacturing push.
events.background: Honda had been building the Prologue, an electric SUV developed jointly with General Motors, at a plant in Mexico for the US market.
events.drivers: Weak US demand for electric vehicles pushed Honda to end Prologue sales starting with the 2026 model year and focus instead on gasoline-hybrid and combustion models.
events.implications: The decision removes a Mexico-made model from Honda's US lineup and signals reduced reliance on EV production from its Mexican plant.
events.next: Prologue production will stop by the end of this year and sales will halt from the 2026 model year.

events.title: Pemex halts oil exports to Cuba for first time in 33 years
events.why: The state oil company stops shipments after Trump administration warnings, a move that signals Mexico's caution in handling energy ties with Havana under US pressure.
events.background: Pemex has sent oil exports to Cuba every year since official records began in 1993, even as the Trump administration pressured Mexico over support for the island.
events.drivers: The value of oil exports to Cuba fell to zero dollars in May, a 100% drop both monthly and annually, amid Trump's threats to intervene against Miguel Diaz-Canel.
events.next: President Sheinbaum said in late June that Mexico will keep sending oil to Cuba despite the threats.

events.title: Slim's Grupo Carso buys 30% stake in Mexican oil field from TotalEnergies
events.why: Carso will take a stake in Block 30, home to the KAN field operated by Harbour Energy, extending the conglomerate's push into Mexico's oil sector as the government seeks private investment in energy.
events.background: Carlos Slim's Grupo Carso has been expanding in Mexico's oil sector as a services contractor to Pemex on assets like Ixachi, Lakach and Macavil.
events.drivers: Carso will buy TotalEnergies' 30% stake in Block 30, home to the KAN field, leaving Harbour Energy as operator with the remaining 70%.
events.next: Closing of the transaction is subject to obtaining the corresponding government authorizations.

events.title: Mexico issues travel advisory for the US over intestinal infection outbreak
events.why: The advisory responds to a rise in cyclosporiasis cases across several US states, an unusual reversal of the typical direction of health warnings between the two countries.

events.title: BlackRock says annual USMCA reviews will bring uncertainty to Mexico investment
events.why: BlackRock does not expect Mexico to lose its sovereign investment-grade rating, but flags the treaty's annual review mechanism as a recurring source of uncertainty for investors in the country.
events.background: The United States recently declined to renew the USMCA trade agreement, prompting BlackRock to warn that Mexico's investment outlook has clouded.
events.drivers: José Luis Ortega of BlackRock México said long-term investors will not move aggressively while uncertainty over annual treaty reviews persists.
events.implications: BlackRock does not expect Mexico to lose its sovereign investment-grade rating, but says high economic growth is unlikely because investment will not arrive in large amounts under continued uncertainty.

events.title: Finance ministry accelerates cuts to productive investment to control fiscal deficit
events.why: Hacienda is cutting spending on productive investment ahead of the second half of the year in an effort to contain the fiscal deficit, according to the report.
events.background: Public finance data through May from the Finance Ministry shows a strategy of fiscal consolidation built on cutting productive investment, with support from cuts at Pemex.
events.drivers: Hacienda, headed by Edgar Amador Zamora, has relied on Pemex investment cuts to ease pressure on the fiscal framework, while spending on social development continues to rise.
events.implications: The SAT has raised revenue through tax credit collections, but the ministry led by Edgar Amador Zamora is relying on investment cuts, particularly at Pemex, to ease fiscal pressure while social development spending keeps rising.

events.title: Regulator CNBV fines BanFeliz amid possible change of control
events.why: The banking regulator issued four fines of 481,100 pesos each against the digital bank, its first sanctions, as the institution appears to be undergoing an ownership change.

events.title: Mexico's imports from Asia grow 42% despite tariffs
events.why: Purchases from Taiwan, China and other Asian countries rose 42.4% through May while trade with the Americas grew only 4.5%, suggesting tariff pressure has not curbed Asian sourcing.
events.background: Mexico applies tariffs to 11 countries without a trade agreement, including China, India, South Korea, Vietnam, Thailand and Taiwan.
events.drivers: Imports from Asia grew 42.55% in January-May 2026 compared with 4.49% growth from the Americas, with Taiwan imports up 233.76% annually.
events.implications: Asian imports rose from 108,971 million dollars to 155,226 million dollars in the period, now representing half of Mexico's total imports, showing tariffs have not curbed sourcing from Asia.

events.title: Toyota's Tacoma displaces Mexico's top vehicle export to the US
events.why: The Tacoma became the single most-exported vehicle from Mexico to the United States in the first half of 2026, according to trade data cited in the report.
events.background: The Tacoma pickup, built at Toyota's Tijuana plant, had been Mexico's top vehicle export to the United States, with Mexico shipping 1.28 million cars to the US in the first half of 2026, a 3.6% annual decline.
events.drivers: Toyota is moving Tacoma production from its Baja California plant to San Antonio, Texas, over an approximate four-year period, with an estimated production increase of 150,000 units there.
events.implications: Toyota exported 140,536 units in the first half of the year, 124,871 of them Tacomas, and Mexico stands to lose this exporting engine as production shifts north.
events.next: The production move to San Antonio will happen gradually over about four years.

events.title: Mexico's M&A deal value more than doubles in first half of 2026
events.why: Mergers and acquisitions reached 10,458 million dollars in the first six months of the year, led by deals involving Fibra Next and Sierra Madre Gold, pointing to renewed corporate dealmaking despite trade uncertainty.
events.background: Mexico's M&A market recorded 90 transactions worth 5,055.2 million dollars in the first half of 2025, according to Seale & Associates.
events.drivers: Investors are concentrating capital in what they consider quality assets, particularly in real estate, industrial and mining sectors, according to Seale & Associates partner Carlos Hernández.
events.implications: Carlos Hernández of Seale & Associates said the market is not slowing but shifting toward larger-scale transactions, with investors concentrating capital in real estate, industrial and mining assets.

events.title: Audio leaks deepen feud between Baja California governor Marina del Pilar Ávila and former governor Jaime Bonilla
events.why: Ávila says the recordings, tied to a meeting with people she believed were US officials, were leaked by Bonilla, and the fallout threatens the ruling coalition's alliance with the PT in the state.
events.background: Marina del Pilar Ávila, the current Baja California governor, has cast former governor Jaime Bonilla as her main political adversary.
events.drivers: Leaked audio appears to show Ávila offering to share security information with people she believed were US officials in exchange for immunity, and she blames Bonilla for recording and leaking the calls.
events.implications: The scandal exposes Ávila to accusations of treason and threatens the ruling coalition's alliance with the PT party in the state.

events.title: USTR delegation to visit Mexico as US pushes to cut trade deficit
events.why: Jamieson Greer says the US wants to shrink its trade deficit with Mexico as USTR sends technical negotiators ahead of the USMCA review, signaling Washington will use the review to press for structural changes in trade flows.
events.background: The USMCA review is underway, and USTR chief Jamieson Greer spoke publicly at the Aspen Security Forum about US goals for the process.
events.drivers: Greer said he has a presidential mandate to control the trade deficit with Mexico through tariffs, quotas or other mechanisms, citing subsidies, overcapacity and unfair trade practices.
events.implications: Washington intends to tighten rules of origin and rebalance production between the two countries to favor US manufacturing relocation, reshaping the terms of the trade relationship beyond simple modernization.

events.title: Rocha Moya case and organized crime complicate USMCA outlook
events.why: The decision to hold annual reviews instead of extending the deal for 16 years is read as political pressure from the Trump administration tied to corruption and cartel cases implicating Mexican officials.
events.background: Rubén Rocha Moya, former governor of Sinaloa, has been implicated in a security case that has strained US-Mexico relations amid broader concerns about cartel activity.
events.drivers: Analyst David Saucedo argues Washington's shift to annual USMCA reviews, instead of a 16-year extension, reflects Trump administration frustration over the lack of action against Rocha Moya and broader security concerns.
events.implications: The security dispute is now shaping trade policy, linking the pace and terms of USMCA review directly to Mexico's handling of organized crime and official corruption cases.

events.title: Auto industry says sectoral tariffs block rules-of-origin talks with US
events.why: The head of Mexico's auto industry association says automakers paying a 19% tariff to Washington cannot negotiate rules of origin while sectoral tariffs remain in place, tying the USMCA review directly to the sector's cost structure.
events.background: The Trump administration refused to renew USMCA for another 16 years and instead activated annual reviews through 2036, with the auto sector at the center of the dispute.
events.next: Garza says negotiators should have something resolved this year.

events.title: New witness in fuel-theft probe says smuggling network sought help from Ojeda Durán
events.why: A witness in the huachicol fiscal investigation alleges the smuggling ring sought assistance involving a contraband ship from Ojeda Durán, the former navy secretary under López Obrador, deepening a case that has already implicated senior officials.
events.background: The witness, Miguel Ángel Solano Ruiz, known as Capitán Sol, told a military officer working at the Guaymas customs post in Sonora that he planned to talk to Ojeda Durán to control the situation involving the Torn Agnes, a ship carrying illegal fuel that arrived at that border facility around the same time as an earlier seizure in Tampico in March 2025. The defense for the Farías Laguna family, who are relatives of Ojeda Durán, has unsuccessfully requested that the former navy secretary testify in the fuel-contraband case. Ojeda Durán served as secretary of the navy under President Andrés Manuel López Obrador.

events.title: Small gas station owners to meet SAT, Sener and Pemex over diesel price cap
events.why: Independent gas station operators want an explanation of how authorities set the 27-peso ceiling on diesel prices, a policy that affects their margins and could signal broader tension over fuel price controls.
events.background: The government set a 27-peso price ceiling on diesel, a policy independent gas station owners say has become unsustainable as it squeezes their margins.
events.implications: The dispute signals ongoing friction over fuel price controls, with station owners skeptical that the government will address their concerns since it maintains the issues are already resolved.
events.next: The meeting is scheduled for next Tuesday in Guadalajara.
events.drivers: About 550 station representatives plan to meet officials from Hacienda, Sener, Pemex, SAT, CNE, ASEA and Profeco after an earlier meeting in Tlaxcala produced little progress on their complaints.

events.title: Mexican stock exchange expects fewer debt issuances after Pemex-driven surge
events.why: Long-term debt issuance grew more than 80% in the first half of the year, a jump analysts call unusual and tied largely to Pemex's return to bond markets, with volumes expected to normalize.
events.background: Pemex returned to the local bond market in February after a pause of more than six years, placing 31,500 million pesos.
events.drivers: Long-term debt issuance on the BMV reached 192,866 million pesos in the first half of 2026, a jump of more than 80% versus the same period in 2025, driven largely by Pemex's large placement.
events.implications: Analysts caution the nominal increase should be read carefully since it depended heavily on a few large operations concentrated early in the year, and expect less dynamism in debt issuance for the second half of 2026.

events.title: Auto sector shift hits Grupo México's rail business
events.why: Grupo México Transportes will lose about 1.5 million dollars a year in revenue after the Tacoma pickup's production changes, showing how shifting auto supply chains ripple into rail freight.
events.background: Grupo México Transportes previously moved Tacoma pickup chassis made by Metalsa in Guanajuato to Mexicali by rail, from where they went by road to Toyota's Tecate plant for final assembly.
events.drivers: Toyota's decision to shift Tacoma production from Baja California to Texas eliminated the need for that rail service, which had moved 30 chassis platforms per week.
events.implications: Vehicles and parts account for 72% of goods exported by rail according to ATTRAPI, so the shift shows how auto manufacturing changes are already cutting into rail operators' revenue.

events.title: Private consumption in Mexico heads toward 15th month of expansion
events.why: Household spending data point to stronger performance in the second quarter, a signal economists watch closely for reading the strength of domestic demand and growth.
events.background: Household spending has been a main pillar of Mexico's economic activity alongside manufacturing exports, according to Monex's Janneth Quiroz.
events.drivers: Inegi's Indicador Oportuno del Consumo Privado points to annual growth of 2.8% in May and 2.6% in June, extending a streak of consecutive gains.
events.implications: If confirmed, the data would extend private consumption's expansion to 15 months, underscoring household spending's role in supporting domestic demand while other parts of the economy show less momentum.

events.title: Sheinbaum says USMCA review enters key phase, seeks deal before September
events.why: Her comments set a rough timeline for resolving the review, a process now complicated by US demands for tariffs and quotas on Mexican exports.

events.title: GAFI warns organized crime increasingly launders money through cryptocurrencies
events.why: The financial crime watchdog says gaps in crypto regulation are being exploited by criminal groups to move large sums, pointing to a regulatory gap Mexico and other countries need to close.

events.title: US tightens visa rules for foreign students, researchers and journalists
events.why: The new limits cap most academic and press visas at four years, part of a broader tightening of entry rules that affects more than two million foreign students and professionals, including Mexicans.

events.title: Geely brings technology transfer program to Tec de Monterrey
events.why: The Chinese automaker will back research projects, vehicle loans and specialized training in smart mobility as part of its push to build a stronger presence in Mexico.

events.title: AT&T asks regulator to limit Telcel's dominance in 5G spectrum auction
events.why: AT&T is interested in the 3.5 GHz band but has asked the CRT to design auction rules that prevent further spectrum concentration with Telcel, the market leader.

events.title: Teachers' union CNTE loses momentum in return to street protests
events.why: The dissident teachers' movement revives demonstrations over what it says are unmet government commitments, but turnout falls well short of the marches that disrupted Mexico City during the World Cup.
